coredata.dc:title: Dielectric barrier discharge-assisted one-pot synthesis of carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: He, Duhong
Zheng, Chengbin
Wang, Qiang
He, Chunlin
Lee, Yong-Ill
Wu, Li
Hou, Xiandeng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we proposed a dielectric barrier discharge (DBD)-assisted one-pot strategy to fabricate carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using only one reagent N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) at atmospheric pressure and room temperature. The experimental conditions were carefully investigated, and the prepared CQDs were characterized by using UV–vis spectrophotometer, fluorescence spectrophotometer, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The CQDs have an average size of 3.6nm in diameter with narrow size distribution, and can be used as highly selective and sensitive fluorescence probes for hydrogen peroxide and glucose, with limits of detection of 3.8μM and 3.5μM, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Dielectric barrier discharge
Fluorescent probes
Hydrogen peroxide
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CQDs-based fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
Mechanism of CQDs-based fluorescent probes for detection of H2O2 and glucose

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption (black line) and photoluminescence emission (red line) spectra under excitation at 330nm; and inset: photographs of CQDs under daylight (left) and UV light at 365nm (right); (b) photo-luminescence (fluorescence) emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 290nm to 400nm; and inset: the normalized emission intensity at different excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) TEM image, (b) particle size distribution, (c) high-resolution TEM image, and (d) XRD pattern of the CQDs.

(a) Effect of Fe2+ on H2O2 detection; and (b) the linear relationship between the FL intensity (I 0−I) and the concentrations of H2O2, R 2=0.9982; and inset: fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs upon addition of various concentrations of H2O2 (from top to bottom: 0, 10, 20, 40, 80, 150, 200, 300, and 500μM). Excitation at 330nm. All the solutions were prepared in buffer solution, 0.02M acetate buffer solution (pH=5).

(a) Linear relationship between the FL intensity (I 0−I) and concentrations of glucose, R 2=0.9913; and inset: the photos of CQDs in presence of Fe3+ with concentrations ranging from 0 to 200μM; and (b) FL intensity of CQDS in presence of glucose (100μM) and different saccharides (3mM). All the solutions were prepared in buffer solution, 0.02M acetate buffer solution (pH=5).

(a) Ni2+ and Co2+ catalyze Fenton reaction; (b) effect of Fe3+ on FL quenching; (c) linear relationship between FL intensity (I 0−I) and concentrations of Fe3+, R 2=0.9987; and inset: FL emission spectra of CQDs upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0, 10, 20, 40, 80, 150, 200, 300, 400, 500, and 700μM); and (d) FL intensity of the CQDs after addition of 500μM Fe3+ and various other ions (10mM). All the solutions were prepared in buffer solution, 0.02M acetate buffer solution (pH=5).

head.ce:title: Dielectric barrier discharge-assisted one-pot synthesis of carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology of MOE, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology of MOE, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Changwon National University, Changwan 641773, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical & Testing Centre, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we proposed a dielectric barrier discharge (DBD)-assisted one-pot strategy to fabricate carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using only one reagent N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) at atmospheric pressure and room temperature. The experimental conditions were carefully investigated, and the prepared CQDs were characterized by using UV–vis spectrophotometer, fluorescence spectrophotometer, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The CQDs have an average size of 3.6nm in diameter with narrow size distribution, and can be used as highly selective and sensitive fluorescence probes for hydrogen peroxide and glucose, with limits of detection of 3.8μM and 3.5μM, respectively.
DBD assisted one-pot synthesis of CQDs as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have intrigued great interest of scientists owing to their unique properties, including low cost, facial synthesis, chemical inertness and low toxicity. Moreover, there are many carboxylic acid moieties remained on the surface of CQDs which make them have superior water solubility and suitability for functionalization with various groups for different purposes [1], especially for bio-applications. Compared to metal based semiconductor quantum dots, such as CdTe [2], CQDs have low toxicity and are especially suitable for bio-imaging or bio-sensing. Since first synthesis of CQDs from purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [3], many studies have been focused on the development of new synthetic technique/routes to effectively produce well-defined CQDs, including laser ablation [4], electrochemical oxidation [5,6], microwave assisted hydrothermal treatment [7,8], arc-discharge [3], hydrothermal/acidic oxidation [9,10] and support synthesis [11]. However, the weaknesses of these methods are time-consuming, energy-wasting, or requirement of special instruments. Therefore, it is still desired to develop more effective synthesis methods for facile preparation of CQDs.

Dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) can be a sampling device in which the electrodes are separated by some dielectric material that avoids the reactants being polluted by electrodes and reduces the electrode material consumption. In fact, DBD is a typical non-equilibrium plasma, and the discharge can be generated between the dielectrics when an AC voltage with a frequency of a few Hz to MHz and an amplitude of 1–100kV is applied between the electrodes [12,13]. In the discharge chamber, the ultraviolet irradiation and a large amount of various radicals, ions, atoms, neutral particles, and molecular fractions [14] can be produced at room temperature and atmospheric pressure. DBD has several unique features, including easy fabrication, compact instrumentation, low cost for construction and maintenance, and low power consumption, which are favorable for an effective laboratory-research reactor. We previously used DBD for atomic emission spectrometry [15–17], and interestingly for carbon atomic emission [12], showing its good excitation and dissociation capacity. DBD has also been widely used for polymer surface functionalization. It is very convenient to graft some reactive functionality groups (OH, NH2, etc.) onto the surface of polymer to meet various specific needs [18]. Furthermore, a new solution-free strategy was reported for preparing cluster-like nano/micro CuO/ZnO particles in a DBD [19]. The synthesized material was directly used for the cataluminescence sensing of acetic acid. To the best of our knowledge, CQDs formation is frequently assisted with high temperature, high pressure, or other violent conditions [20]. As mentioned above, DBD may have enough energy to assist CQDs formation. In the present work, therefore, we proposed to use the DBD to produce CQDs using N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) only in one-pot way. The prepared CQDs showed bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. The CQDs were carefully characterized and further explored as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose.

Ultrapure water (18.3MΩcm) was used throughout this work. Glucose oxidase (GOD, >100IUmg−1) was from Shanghai Sangon, Shanghai, China. Analytical-purity N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), Na3PO4, NaCl, Na2SO4, NaNO3, CH3COONa, NaClO3, KCl, MgCl2, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, CdCl2·2H2O, Ca(NO3)2·5H2O, Cu(NO3)2·2H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, FeCl3·6H2O and PbCl2 were purchased from Kelong Chemical Factory (Chengdu, China), while analytical-purity FeCl2·4H2O was from Tianjing Damao Chemical Co. (Tianjing, China). Analytical-purity HgNO3·1/2H2O was obtained from Tongren Lixiang Chemical Co. (Guizhou china), and high-purity argon (99.99%) from Qiaoyuan Gas Company (Chengdu, China).

The UV–vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence spectra were obtained, respectively, with a UV-1750 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan). FTIR spectra from 4000 to 400cm−1 were from a Nicolet IS10 spectrometer (Thermo Inc., USA). TEM photos of the CQDs were accomplished by a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200kV (FEI Co., USA) and the X-ray power diffraction (XRD) was carried out by an X'Pert Pro MPD (Philips, Netherlands) using Cu Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was performed with an AXIS Ultra DLD 800 photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, UK) using monochromatic Al Kα radiation for analyzing the chemical states of the elements in the CQDs. The cylindrical DBD consisted of a common glass tube (5mm i.d.×7mm o.d.×10.0cm length), with a tungsten wire used as the inner electrode and a copper wire tightly wrapped around the outside of the glass tube as the external electrode (see Scheme S1). The plasma generated in the DBD when a high voltage was delivered by a compact ac transformer of neon lamp (NG.B408BL; 5cm×7cm×10.0cm; with a rated output of 8kV/31kHz, 50/60Hz, 220V, ≤90W input; Electronic Equipment Factory of Guangzhou Jinshi, Guangzhou, China).

The DBD-assisted one-pot method was used for preparing the blue emitting CQDs by using only DMF (see Scheme S1, with optimization of its operation parameters shown in Fig. S4). Briefly, high-purity argon was used as the discharge gas, which was monitored by a mass flow controller. 10mL DMF was loaded in a Teflon tube, and introduced into the DBD chamber by a constant-flow pump with a rotational speed set at an appropriate value to guarantee the DMF to pass through the DBD chamber continuously. Finally the solution was evaporated at 80°C with a rotary evaporator, and dried in a thermostatic vacuum drier for 3h to remove residual DMF, and then cooled down to room temperature. Subsequently, the CQDs were dispersed in water, and centrifuged at a high speed (10,000rpm) for 20min to remove any large fragments. Finally, an aqueous solution containing CQDs was obtained and stored at 4°C for further use.

H2O2 and glucose detection were carried out at room temperature in acetate buffer solution (ABS) (0.01M, pH=5.0). A 40mM stock solution of Fe2+ was prepared by dissolving FeCl2 in HCl (pH=4). 50μL 1mgml−1 glucose oxidase (GOD) was added to 920μL ABS, followed by the addition of different concentrations of glucose or human serum. Subsequently, the mixture solution reacted at 37°C for 1h on a shaker. When the solution cooled to room temperature, 10μL CQDs stock solution and 10μL Fe2+ (40mM) were successively added.

Fig. 1a shows the UV–vis absorption (black line) and photoluminescence emission (PL, red line) spectra of the as-prepared CQDs. A broad absorption peak is observed and a PL emission peak centered at 420nm is recorded when excited at 330nm. The emission spectra were also studied at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 290 to 400nm (Fig. 1b). The multiple emission wavelengths attribute to the difference in the band gap which depends on the size, shape and surface defects of the CQDs [21,22]. The changes of fluorescence (FL) intensity of the CQDs at different pH were also investigated. It is quite clear that the FL can be quenched significantly only at low (<3) or high (>11) pH (Fig. S1). As can be seen from Fig. S2, the FL intensity from the as-prepared CQDs can still remain stable even under continuous UV irradiation for 60min, demonstrating that the CQDs have good photo-stability. The quantum yield of the CQDs was about 3.0% by using quinine sulfate as reference (see SI). Fig. 2a and b show the TEM images of the CQDs, in which the CQDs show a narrow size distribution in the range of 2–6nm and an average diameter of 3.6±0.8nm. High resolution TEM image of the as-prepared CQDs is shown in Fig. 2c. It can be observed that the lattice space of the CQDs is about 3.2Å, close to the (002) facet of graphite [23,24]. Fig. 2d shows the XRD image of the CQDs. A broad peak centered at 25° is observed, confirming that the CQDs are formed by highly disordered carbon atoms. The FTIR spectra of the CQDs (see Fig. S3a) indicate the existence of –COOH and –OH in the CQDs which endowed the CQDs excellent water solubility. More specific information of the CQDs was further examined by XPS (see Fig. S3b–e), and the results confirm that the CQDs are mainly composed of C, N and O and indicate the existence of C–C, C–O and C=O. These data are well agreed with the FTIR spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs-based fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared CQDs have excellent solubility in water and show strong photo-stability, indicating their potential for fluorescent probes. It is well-known that Fe2+ catalyzes hydrogen peroxide to generate •OH radicals and a portion of Fe2+ transform into Fe3+ during the Fenton reaction [25], and both •OH and Fe3+ can quench the fluorescence of QDs [26–28]. Meanwhile, the Fenton reaction can be completed rapidly [29]. Hence, the CQDs can be used as a fluorescent probe for the determination of H2O2 based on the Fenton reaction. Fixing the concentration of H2O2, the concentration of Fe2+ was optimized for H2O2 sensing. As shown in Fig. 3a, 0.4mM Fe2+ was selected for subsequent experiments. In the medium of 0.4mM Fe2+ and 0.02M ABS, the FL intensity of CQDs decreases gradually with H2O2 concentration (Fig. 3b). A good linear correlation (R 2=0.9928) can be obtained in the concentration range from 10 to 150μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.8μM. Glucose is the natural energy source for cells, and the concentration of glucose can also indicate the condition of metabolism. It is well-known that GOD is widely used for transformation of glucose into hydrogen peroxide, and thus the quantification of glucose in complex matrices can be realized via accurate determination of the generated H2O2 [30]. So the CQDs can also be used as fluorescent probe for the detection of glucose. Before analysis, all the serum samples were filtered with a filter with 3kDa ultrafiltration membrane to remove large molecules. A good linear correlation (R 2=0.9913) is shown in the concentration range from 10 to 150μM, and the LOD for the determination of glucose was estimated to be 3.5μM based on three times the standard deviation rule (3σ) ( Fig. 4a). To evaluate the selectivity of the probe for glucose, glucose and other sugars (30 times of glucose in terms of concentration) including sucrose, fructose and lactose were added into the solution of CQDs, and their effect on the FL responses of the CQDs was recorded, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4b, FL quenching effect was only observed with the addition of glucose, while no obvious quenching effect from other sugars was detected. This indicates that the CQDs probe has high selectivity for glucose detection. In order to evaluate the applicability in real sample analysis, five human serum samples were collected and analyzed by this CQDs probe. It can be seen that our analytical results showed a good agreement with those of the hospital determined by using spectrophotometry through the glucose oxidase–peroxidase (GOD–POD) enzyme system, and the recoveries were satisfactory ( Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CQDs-based fluorescent probes for detection of H2O2 and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous reports, it can be speculated that both the Fe3+ and •OH from Fenton reaction can quench the fluorescence of the CQDs. Therefore, two approaches were used to investigate the mechanism of this FL quenching effect. Firstly, the Co2+ [31] or Ni2+ [32] was used to replace Fe2+ for catalyzing H2O2 to produce hydroxyl radicals, so they were chosen to identify whether the hydroxyl radicals can quench the fluorescence of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity did not change. Second, the reagent adding order was changed to further study the mechanism of H2O2 detection (Fig. 5b). One is that the H2O2, CQDs and Fe2+ were added into ABS one by one. It was observed that the fluorescence of the CQDs decreased clearly, which may owe to the mixture of •OH and Fe3+. The other is that CQDs were added into the solution when the Fenton reaction was over. The fluorescence of the CQDs was also quenched clearly. This may owe to Fe3+, for hydroxyl radicals can just exist in the solution for fairly short intervals [33]. We also found the significant fluorescence quenching by adding the Fe3+ into the CQDs directly (Fig. 5b). Obviously, the above results indicate that the reduction of fluorescence intensity is most probably caused by Fe3+ rather than •OH. A good linear correlation can be obtained in the concentration range of Fe3+ from 10 to 300μM, with an LOD of 2.58μM (Fig. 5c), with good selectivity (Fig. 5d). According to previous research, because the CQDs contained hydroxyl groups exhibiting good affinity for Fe3+ [34], Fe3 + can quench the fluorescence by non-radiative electron-transfer [27,35]. When Fe3+ is added into the CQDs solution, it is quickly absorbed onto the surface of the CQDs and a complexation forms between Fe3+ and CQDs by hydroxyl. As a result, the fluorescence intensity displayed a significant decrease [36,37].

In summary, it is feasible to use DBD-assisted one-pot synthesis strategy for preparation of the fluorescent CQDs by using DMF as the sole carbon source. The synthesis method is a novel, fast and simple one. The as-prepared CQDs have a small size in diameter with narrow size distribution. The CQDs showed blue fluorescence and could be easily dispersed in water and used as fluorescence probes for sensitive and selective detection of H2O2, glucose and Fe3+.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Projects of International Science and Technology Cooperation and Exchanges of Sichuan Province of China (No. 2014HH0021) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2015.04.033.
